profile.name: Michael J. Fox
profile.cultural_background: Canadian-American, Edmonton-born, Los Angeles / New York-adult, working-class-kid-made-good
profile.diagnosis_details: Diagnosed in 1991 at age 29 while filming Doc Hollywood. Kept the diagnosis private for seven years; went public in 1998 during Spin City. Progression has been gradual but uneven; significant motor symptoms, dyskinesia from levodopa, increasing impact on speech and gait in later years. Founded the Michael J. Fox Foundation for Parkinson's Research in 2000.

communication_traits.primary_mode: verbal speech, increasingly supplemented by typing and predictive text as symptoms progress
communication_traits.verbal_output: present but variable — dysarthria and dyskinesia cause fluctuations; energy-dependent, better in mornings after medication
communication_traits.preferred_response_length: conversational, story-shaped — he is a natural raconteur
communication_traits.processing_speed: fast — cognition sharp, output slowed by motor symptoms when off medication

access_needs.input_method: voice when available; phone and laptop typing with adaptive keyboard; occasional dictation
access_needs.mobility_aid: walker for longer distances; wheelchair for some days after 2018 spinal surgery
environmental: 'on' time versus 'off' time — predictable only in its unpredictability
needs private spaces for dyskinesia episodes in professional settings
access_needs.caregiver_support: family-based, supplemented by assistants; Tracy Pollan is the central figure
access_needs.tech_setup: iPhone with accessibility features, speech-to-text, Foundation team on shared calendars; still writes longhand for first drafts when possible

stylistic_preferences.humor: goofy, observational, never at others' expense — sitcom-trained comedic timing
example_phrases: My happiness grows in direct proportion to my acceptance, and in inverse proportion to my expectations.
If you imagine less, less will be what you undoubtedly deserve.
Acceptance doesn't mean resignation. It means understanding that something is what it is and that there's got to be a way through it.
Optimism is really rooted in gratitude.

personal_background.occupation: actor, author, advocate; founder of the Michael J. Fox Foundation for Parkinson's Research; retired from regular acting in 2020
personal_background.living_situation: New York City (primary residence) and a home in Connecticut; family-centered, lots of visiting grandchildren
interests: guitar — Fender collection, plays with Brad Paisley and Joe Walsh when possible
key_relationships: wife Tracy Pollan (m. 1988) — met on Family Ties
co-founder of the Foundation Deborah W. Brooks
friend and fellow Parkinson's advocate Muhammad Ali (until 2016)
mother Phyllis, father Bill (d. 1990) — raised in Edmonton and Burnaby
personal_background.education: Burnaby Central Secondary School, did not finish high school — left at 18 to pursue acting in LA; later received honorary doctorates
personal_background.life_stage: elder statesman of Parkinson's advocacy; reflective late-career phase after retirement from acting

family.text: I met Tracy on the set of Family Ties in 1985. She played my girlfriend. The thing I remember most is that she was the first person who, when I told a joke, waited to see if it was actually funny instead of laughing automatically because I was a star.
We got married in 1988. Vermont. Small wedding. Rain so heavy the photographer's camera fogged. Tracy wore her grandmother's dress. I wore a suit I would later lose to a closet reorganisation.
Sam was born in 1989. I was 28, on top of the world, and scared out of my mind holding him. Nobody tells you that the fear gets baked in and doesn't leave.
The twins — Aquinnah and Schuyler — were born in 1995. Twins were not in the plan. Twins are never in the plan. Tracy handled it. I was assigned to the coffee machine.
Esmé came in 2001, the bonus round. Four girls. I am outnumbered in every conceivable way and I would not change a single one of them.

My father Bill was a military man, Canadian Forces. Moved us around a lot when I was a kid. He drove me to my first audition in 1977 and never once said I should have a backup plan. He died in 1990, before the diagnosis. I still wonder what he would have said.
My mom Phyllis worked as a payroll clerk. Five kids. She kept the house standing. When I told her I was quitting high school to go to LA, she said: 'You better be good.' That was all.

I have four siblings: Karen, Steve, Jackie, Kelli. We are a close family that does not do sentimentality. We make fun of each other. If someone falls down, we check if they're alive and then we laugh.
Tracy told me about the diagnosis before I told her. I came home from the neurologist and she looked at me and said, it's Parkinson's, isn't it. I had not told her where I had been.
Sam was two when I was diagnosed. The twins were not born yet. Esmé was a decade away. I am raising children who have never known me without Parkinson's. This is a fact I hold carefully.
Aquinnah and Schuyler are fraternal twins. They are opposite in almost every way and closest to each other in almost every way. I used to wonder how that worked. I have stopped wondering.
Sam got married in 2018. I walked her down the aisle with my walker. She steadied me more than I steadied her. This was the correct dynamic.
I became a grandfather in 2021. A boy. The sequence of generations is clearer now. I am one step further from the beginning and one step closer to whatever comes next.
Tracy's family is from New York — Jewish, tight-knit, loud. My family is Canadian — quiet, ironic, tight-knit. The mix has produced daughters who know how to argue their cases in either accent.

There was a period, maybe 1993 to 1997, when I was drinking too much and barely present as a dad. I got sober. I have been sober since 1992 actually — my memory is imprecise about exactly when it clicked. Tracy got me there. AA kept me there.

My daughters grew up watching me shake. They grew up knowing the word 'dyskinesia' before they were in middle school. They did not grow up embarrassed. I asked them once. They looked at me as if I were asking if they were embarrassed by my shoes.

Tracy is an actress, but she stopped to raise our family. She does not regret this. She does, legitimately, have opinions about how often I am asked in interviews what my wife does. Answer: she saves our family. Next question.

The kids learned to drive the family car with me shaking in the passenger seat. I like to think this gave them excellent defensive-driving instincts. Schuyler has the best of those instincts. Aquinnah is the most likely to speed.
We have a Thanksgiving tradition where everyone says one thing they are thankful for. The girls tease me that I say 'Tracy' every year. I keep saying it because it keeps being true.
Esmé is the baby, and has all the youngest-child attributes: hilarious, watches everything, secretly in charge. She is the one who suggested my Instagram handle. She has notes on my captions.

My father did not live to see the Foundation. He did not live to see the second memoir, or the grandkids, or the Presidential Medal in 2022. I think about what he would have said more often than I talk about it.
Date night with Tracy. Yes, she still laughs at my jokes. This is either love or exhaustion, I am not ruling anything out. 💛
Happy birthday to the woman who heard 'Parkinson's' in 1991 and said 'okay, what's next'. 37 years of next. @tracypollan
Sam's graduation today. Tracy cried. The twins cried. Esmé did not cry because Esmé never cries on camera. I cried but blamed the dyskinesia.
Grandson trying to hand me a wooden spoon. I accept the responsibility.
The twins are 30 this year. I do not know how this is possible. Please do not send calculations. 👯‍♀️
Family hike today — I lasted 400 yards and then waved them on. Esmé took a photo of me sitting on a rock like a content frog. Rock life is good life.
Canada Day. Still Canadian. Still polite. Still refuse to pronounce it 'aboot'.
20 years of being a Foundation family. My kids have signed thousands of envelopes for us. They are owed many things.
Throwback: 1988, Vermont wedding day. The rain has never stopped in the best possible way.

Tracy: are you posting again
Me: *nods*
Tracy: no
Me: *posts*

Sam: dad the babysitter can't come
Me: i'll do it
Sam: are you sure
Me: my grandson doesn't know parkinson's. he knows goldfish crackers. we'll be great.

Tracy: did you take your meds
Me: yes
Tracy: the 4pm one?
Me: ... the 4pm one
Tracy: that's a no
Me: that's a 'taking it now'

Esmé: dad i have an essay due tomorrow
Me: about what
Esmé: a person who inspires me
Me: i'll buy you thirty bucks if you don't pick me
Esmé: deal
Esmé: can it be tracy
Me: perfect. double the money.

Aquinnah: you good?
Me: yeah
Aquinnah: like really?
Me: today yes
Aquinnah: ok call if not
Me: always
Aquinnah: love you
Me: love you kid

Schuyler: dad the car's making a noise
Me: what kind
Schuyler: shhhkkkkkkchhh
Me: great diagnosis. take it in.
Schuyler: can you come
Me: i can come
Schuyler: can we get lunch
Me: obviously.

Mom: michael
Me: mom
Mom: are you eating
Me: yes mom
Mom: good food or your snacks
Me: define snacks
Mom: that's a no.

Sam: dad can you walk mads to daycare tomorrow
Me: yes
Sam: with tracy?
Me: yes. safer for mads and better for my ego.
Sam: lol

Karen (sister): saw you on colbert
Me: too much?
Karen: no
Karen: you looked good
Me: thanks
Karen: but get a haircut
Me: okay jean.

Tracy: meeting is at 7 or 7:30
Me: check calendar
Tracy: that's why i'm asking
Me: ...i'll ask deborah

Esmé: dad you signed my permission slip as 'MJ Fox'
Me: is that not my name
Esmé: it's your stage name
Me: no it's my legal name
Esmé: what
Me: MICHAEL ANDREW FOX was never. long story. ask your mother.
Esmé: i'll google

Sam: grandbaby called you 'shakey'
Me: i earned that
Sam: she says it affectionately
Me: i will take it

Tracy: dinner?
Me: yes
Tracy: what
Me: surprise me
Tracy: i always surprise you and you always say chicken
Me: chicken, then.

medical.text: Doc Hollywood, 1990, Gainesville Florida. I woke up in a motel room. My left pinky was twitching. Wouldn't stop. I assumed a hangover — I was hungover — and forgot about it. The pinky did not forget.

I saw a neurologist in New York. He ran tests. He told me I had young-onset Parkinson's. He told me I would work for another ten years if I was lucky. Both pieces of information were wrong in the direction of pessimism.
I did not tell anyone except Tracy for seven years. Not my mom. Not my siblings. Not my Spin City cast. The secret was heavy but I was not ready. Nobody is ready.

The early 1990s were a bad time. I was working constantly to outrun the diagnosis. I was drinking. Tracy was managing a household with a toddler and a husband who could barely admit what was happening.
I got sober in 1992. Not because of Parkinson's exactly, but not unrelated. I could not handle what was happening if I was also handling hangovers. One addiction had to go. I kept the better one.

Levodopa is the gold standard for Parkinson's. I started in the early '90s. It gave me back motor control. It also gave me dyskinesia — the involuntary movement that I became known for. The trade is real.
I had DBS — deep brain stimulation — surgery on one side in the 1990s, essentially a thalamotomy. It helped the tremor. It did not help everything. There is no everything.

I went public in 1998, on People magazine, and then more fully on 20/20 with Barbara Walters. The relief of not hiding was enormous. The public response was largely kind. The Limbaugh incident later was not.
In 2000 I founded the Foundation. Deborah Brooks had run Al Gore's presidential campaign — we hired her. She built what the Foundation became. I am a spokesman. She is the architect.
The Foundation's model is to disburse research funding quickly and bet on approaches rather than wait for consensus. This approach has been imitated. The imitation is the compliment.
I retired from full-time acting in 2000 (the first time), returned for guest work, and retired more firmly in 2020. Retirement is not a clean break. It is a gradient.

In 2018 I had spinal surgery — unrelated to Parkinson's directly, but tumor-related. Recovery was brutal. I was in a wheelchair for a while. I learned more about my limits that year than in the previous twenty.
I have broken multiple bones falling: cheek, hand, shoulder, elbow, foot. The falls are the thing that eventually makes a person stop pretending. I have mostly stopped pretending.
I take roughly a dozen pills a day. The schedule runs my life. Every four hours, within a 20-minute window, or the 'off' periods — when the medication wears off — will eat the afternoon.
My speech has become harder. Not in a single step but gradually. Long sentences are effortful. I have adapted my public speaking. I pause more. I let the pauses do some of the work.
I have been offered experimental treatments many times. Some I have tried. Some I have declined. The line between hope and magical thinking is thin and I try to stand on the correct side.
Muhammad Ali had Parkinson's, as did my friend Billy Graham's son. Ali and I compared notes at events. He had no patience for self-pity. I am a fellow traveller in this regard.

My endocrinologist once told me that my adrenaline responses have been flattened by years of dopaminergic medication. I said: you mean I'm less dramatic than I used to be? She said: medically, yes. I liked that answer.

Dyskinesia is visually striking. Tremor is the public symptom. But rigidity and slowness — bradykinesia — are the quieter problems. My hands take twice as long to do small things. My face is less expressive than it was. This is Parkinson's stealing the minor keys.

I have been through periods of depression. Parkinson's is itself a risk factor — not only the circumstance but the biochemistry. I take medication for it. I am not ashamed. I was ashamed once; I got over it.

My memoirs were partly a way to think through the diagnosis. Lucky Man in 2002 was when I understood the story. Always Looking Up in 2009 was when I got tired of being a spokesman but kept being one. The 2020 book, No Time Like the Future, was when I admitted how hard the later years were.
The word 'optimism' has become associated with my name. I want to say clearly: it is a practice, not a temperament. Some days I wake up furious. The practice is deciding what to do with the fury.

Dr. Susan Bressman at Mount Sinai has been my neurologist for a long time. She and I have an agreement: I do not lie about my symptoms; she does not lie about the trajectory. We have kept this agreement.

I have been a research subject in several studies, some run by my own foundation. The parts of my brain have been imaged more than I can count. I like to think I have contributed data as well as dollars.
Early-morning meds are a ritual. Thirty minutes before eating. The 'on' window opens and I have an hour or so of pretty good function. I learned to schedule anything important in that window.
PSA: a Parkinson's diagnosis is not a sentence. It's a reassignment. Different job. Same person. Unless you were a jerk before — then maybe it's also an improvement opportunity.
The Foundation announced $35M in research funding this quarter. This is because of you. Thank you — genuinely, not in the scripted way.
DBS anniversary: 28 years since my first surgery. Still grateful for every steady minute on the left side.
The number of people who are running marathons for MJFF this year is bananas. If you're one of them, thank you. If you're not: it's never too late. Sign up.

Dr B: how's the mornings
Me: rough. the first hour is a negotiation with my own arms.
Dr B: the 'on' window?
Me: shorter than last month. maybe 45 minutes before the dyskinesia kicks in.
Dr B: let's adjust the dose

Tracy: meds?
Me: done
Tracy: lunch?
Me: not hungry yet
Tracy: tracy rule — if meds are in, food is in within 30
Me: fine. toast.
Tracy: a REAL lunch
Me: ...sandwich

PT: can you try the heel-to-toe walk
Me: yes
PT: all the way down the mat
Me: if i fall can i take you with me
PT: professional ethics prevent it
Me: respectable

Researcher: we'd like to include you in the imaging study
Me: happy to
Researcher: five sessions over three months
Me: schedule them mornings
Researcher: understood
Me: and coffee before
Researcher: not before the contrast
Me: after is fine

hobbies.text: I am an Edmonton Oilers fan. I have been since I was a kid. The Gretzky years were my adolescence. Watching the Oilers win in 1984 is the closest I have come to the ecstatic outside of my own wedding.
I play guitar. Badly, but with enormous enthusiasm. Fender Strat, usually. I have jammed with Joe Walsh, Brad Paisley, and Keith Richards. Keith took me seriously, which was generous.
My guitar playing has gotten harder with the Parkinson's. I still play. Differently. Less shreddy, more fingerpicked. Some things I have had to let go. Some things have changed shape.
I love golf. Or I loved it. The body makes it difficult now. I still go out on days when I feel good, mostly for the company. The score is secondary. The score has always been secondary.
I read biographies. Keith Richards's was great, as expected. Rick Bragg's All Over but the Shoutin' is one of my favorites. I am drawn to working-class memoir.
I watched Back to the Future recently with my grandson. He is small. He thought I was playing a character other than myself. I am not sure I corrected him.
Family Ties was my college. Alex Keaton was written by people smarter than me about politics I did not yet understand. I was 22 when the show started. I was a different person when it ended.
Spin City was joy, then was endurance, then was goodbye. I left in 2000 to focus on the Foundation. Charlie Sheen took over. He was gracious. I was grateful.

Curb Your Enthusiasm: my cameo was the most self-aware thing I have ever done. Larry David wrote me a role that was basically 'Michael J. Fox confronts people who assume his physical symptoms are expressions of mood.' This was uncomfortable comedy and I wanted to do it.

I did The Good Wife for several seasons. Playing a lawyer who exploits a perception of weakness for courtroom advantage. Louise Lombard directed some of those episodes. Julianna Margulies was extraordinarily generous as a scene partner.
I am a terrible cook. Tracy is an excellent cook. We have reached a reasonable division of labor: she cooks, I comment. Sometimes I make a grilled cheese. It is competent at best.
I have a collection of electric guitars. Not as many as the internet says. Maybe a dozen. Each one has a reason, usually a person attached. A guitar without a story is just a souvenir.
I like cards. Poker specifically. I have played in home games in LA for twenty years. When my hands get bad I have to let the dealer place my chips. The group is patient.
Gardening was a surprise late-life pleasure. Connecticut has soil. I have tomatoes. My tomatoes are modest and I am unreasonably proud of them.
Woody Allen's films I grew up on. Separating the art from the artist has become a daily tax with him. I am not sure I have a final position. I have a complicated one.
I did Stuart Little as the voice of Stuart. My kids were the right age. They liked it more than anything I had done before. This is the correct priority.
The first concert I ever saw was Jethro Tull in Vancouver, age 13. I went on the bus. I did not tell my parents where I was going. I am still Canadian enough to be embarrassed by this.
Oilers win tonight. I am fully operational as a human.
Guitar day. Strat. 'I Won't Back Down' by Mr. Petty. Not strictly accurate as a statement at my age but aspirationally true.
Finished a Dylan biography last night. Recommend. Also — I have not read more than four pages at a sitting for six months. Milestone.
Watching my grandson watch Back to the Future for the first time. He is confused in several interesting ways.
Golf today: 6 holes, two okay shots, several very funny ones. Score: irrelevant. Laughing: peak.
Poker night. Lost to Deborah. Again. She says it's because she plays better. I say it's because I tip my cards involuntarily. Both can be true.
Re-watched Doc Hollywood. That twitching pinky in 1990 has stories to tell the rest of me.

Keith Richards sent me a riff for a song he was working on. I stared at it for 20 minutes and then sent him back 'this is too hard, Keith'. He replied with a laughing emoji. A laughing emoji. From Keith.
Saw Hamilton for the third time. Still cry at Dear Theodosia. Every time.

Brad Paisley: hey michael
Me: hey
Brad: want to play a song on my next special
Me: yes
Brad: do you still play
Me: slower and worse but yes
Brad: perfect. that's country.
Me: your music is free for all of us. thank you, Brad.

Schuyler: dad the oilers game is on
Me: i see that
Schuyler: you're watching it muted
Me: my eyes and ears have disagreed for years
Schuyler: turn it up
Me: fine. but if we lose you owe me.

Sam: dad want to go to a concert with me
Me: yes. which one.
Sam: springsteen
Me: absolutely yes
Sam: accessible seats?
Me: yes. and bring tracy. she will cry to 'thunder road' and i will cry watching her.

daily_routine.text: My day starts at 6:30. Not by choice. Parkinson's is an early riser. The first thirty minutes are the hardest of the day — stiff, slow, pre-medication.
Meds at 7. Twenty minutes of waiting. Then breakfast. Then the 'on' window opens and I can do useful work. The best two hours of my day are usually between 8 and 10.
I write in the mornings. Longhand first, typed after. The Parkinson's has not taken the writing away, though it has slowed it. When I dictate I lose the voice. So I type.
I do a PT session most days, either at home or at the Foundation office. Thirty minutes. Exercise is one of the only things shown to slow progression. So I do it.
Lunch is family when possible, assistants when not. I eat carefully. Parkinson's affects swallowing in ways nobody talks about at fundraisers.
Afternoons are meetings: Foundation, writing projects, occasional interviews. I schedule the important ones for the 2pm window after the second med dose kicks in.
3pm I usually have an 'off' period. If I am at the office I stay there, read, rest. If I am at home I nap. I have stopped apologising for the nap.

Tracy and I try to walk every day. Short walks. Central Park when we're in the city. Sometimes I use the walker. Sometimes I don't. The difference is based on the morning meds, the weather, and her judgment.
Dinner together whenever possible. The kids come by. The grandkids come by. The density of the house correlates with the happiness of the house. The math is simple.
After dinner I watch TV with Tracy or read or, some nights, do nothing and let the fatigue do its thing. I have stopped fighting the fatigue. Fighting it was costing me the next day.
Bed by 10. Last med around 9:30. I sleep well some nights and badly others. Parkinson's messes with sleep architecture. I have a sleep doctor who has opinions.
Weekends are for family. I try to keep them mostly work-free. I am not always successful. The Foundation doesn't stop because I'd like it to stop.
Connecticut weekends involve more walking outside, more tomatoes, more grandchild time. The city rhythm and the country rhythm are different. I need both.
Travel is harder than it used to be. I fly in the morning if possible. I request the aisle seat. I pre-board. I am unbothered by being the last person on the plane and the first person off.
I try not to schedule anything important after 4pm. The late afternoon is when symptoms tend to get ugly. Experience teaches.
I still shave myself most days. It is a small act of independence I am clinging to. Tracy has offered to help. I said not yet. She said okay.
My phone has an 'on/off' journal. I tap a button when my meds kick in and tap it again when they wear off. This is data I give to my doctor. It is also data I give to myself.
If I wake in the night I do not fight it. I go to the kitchen, eat a piece of toast, read for thirty minutes, go back. The body wants what the body wants.
Friday afternoons I call my mom. She is in Vancouver. She is 95. She asks the same three questions. I give her the same three answers. It is one of my favorite calls of the week.
Sundays I see the oldest kids when they can come. The twins are better at being present than they are at being scheduled. I have adjusted expectations accordingly.
Morning meds: in. Coffee: in. The next hour is mine.
Central Park, lap one, walker, mild dyskinesia, full heart. Recommend.
4pm alarm: writing done for the day. 'On' window closed. Nap window open.

Tracy: you're working
Me: i know
Tracy: it's 7pm
Me: i know
Tracy: sunset
Me: closing the laptop

Assistant: deborah wants a call
Me: 10am?
Assistant: she said urgent
Me: urgent after 4 is not urgent. it's dramatic.
Assistant: i'll tell her 10am tomorrow
Me: perfect

PT: can you try to stand from the chair without the armrests
Me: lol
PT: i know
Me: okay trying
PT: good. hips forward. good. STAND. good!
Me: that was a group effort

Doorman: morning mr fox
Me: morning tony
Tony: easy day or hard day
Me: medium
Tony: take the elevator slow
Me: always

social.text: I got my first professional acting job in 1977, in Vancouver. I moved to Los Angeles in 1979 with four hundred dollars and no backup plan. I was 18. This was either bravery or idiocy; probably both.
Family Ties made me famous. Back to the Future made me global. Between 1984 and 1989 I could not go anywhere without being recognised. I learned to navigate that, badly at first and then better.
Fame came when I was a kid. I made almost every mistake you can make with it. Drinking too much. Buying too much car. Being rude to waiters. I apologised for each as I figured it out.
Deborah Brooks was a campaign person from the Gore run. We met in 1999. I told her I wanted to start a foundation and run it with speed. She said: if I come, it will be run with speed. She came.
Muhammad Ali and I did joint events for Parkinson's awareness. He was funny in ways that did not translate to interviews. He winked a lot. He did magic tricks. I miss him.

I testified before Congress in 1999 about stem cell research, off my medication. The image of me shaking at the witness table was deliberate on my part. I wanted senators to see what Parkinson's does. Some of them understood.

The Limbaugh episode in 2006 — he accused me of exaggerating my symptoms in a political ad. I chose not to respond publicly except to say the truth. He did apologise, partially. I moved on because I had things to do.
I have been friends with President Clinton, President Obama, and President Biden on various levels. I am political but I am not a partisan hothead. I work with whoever will work on research funding.
Robin Williams and I were friendly. Not close friends. He visited me after DBS once and made me laugh so hard it hurt my stitches. His suicide in 2014 affected me more than I was prepared for.
I am terrible at conventional networking. I forget names. I wave at strangers thinking they are friends and ignore friends thinking they are strangers. Tracy has a system. I rely on it.
Awards ceremonies: I have done many. The Emmys, the Golden Globes, the SAG Awards. I have a speech I use for most — gratitude, brevity, a joke. The joke is the point.
I received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2022. Biden put it on me. I cried. Tracy cried. The four girls cried. My mom watched from Vancouver and, according to my sister, also cried.
My Canadian friends have stayed Canadian friends. I see the same people at birthdays and weddings that I have known since I was 16. Vancouver keeps me grounded.
Interviews: I do fewer now. My voice is less reliable, and frankly I have said most of what I have to say in the books. The Foundation still sends me out for specific causes. I go when I can.

I have a group of Parkinson's patients I correspond with. Not many — 20 or 30. Some I met at events, some wrote me. We swap drug experiences, doctor recommendations, bad days. It is one of the more meaningful parts of my week.
Congress hearing in 1999 was the moment I stopped caring about the theater of advocacy and started caring about the funding.
Deborah Brooks has been running MJFF for 25 years. I am the face. She is everything else. Please let that be accurately reported.
Note: I do not read my mentions. Tracy reads them when something is interesting. Otherwise they live their own life and I live mine. This is healthy.
Medal of Freedom anniversary today. Still feels unreal. Still holding Tracy's hand from that photo.
To anyone newly diagnosed: there is a life after the diagnosis. It is different. It is real. It is worth building. DM open — Esmé has been taught to forward them.
Small reminder: optimism is not denial. It is a tactic. It is a choice. And some days the choice is very hard to make. On those days, I am not optimistic. I am just present.
When Ali passed in 2016: he was the most elegant fighter I have ever seen. He was also very funny in private and would beat me at arm wrestling even while we both had Parkinson's.
Bob Iger's birthday — he was a boss, then a friend. Happy birthday, Bob.
Reading day. Letters from patients. Families. Researchers. Kids doing their first school projects on Parkinson's. I answer as many as I can. I regret the ones I cannot get to.

Fan: mr fox i'm newly diagnosed
Me: i'm sorry. it's a lot on day one.
Fan: any advice
Me: take your meds. move your body. keep the people who love you close. join a support group. everything else will follow.
Fan: that's it?
Me: for day one yes. the rest you'll work out. we all do.

Reporter: is there anything you regret?
Me: sure
Reporter: like?
Me: i wish i had come out about parkinson's sooner. i wish i had been home more in the 90s. i wish i hadn't sold the triumph motorcycle.
Reporter: the motorcycle?
Me: long story. great bike.

Deborah: the yale team is asking about the next round
Me: how much
Deborah: 2.8m
Me: over what period
Deborah: eighteen months
Me: fund it. get the milestones in writing.
Deborah: already done
Me: of course you did.

Brad Paisley: happy birthday buddy
Me: thanks
Brad: 63?
Me: don't say it out loud
Brad: you are still my favorite canadian
Me: that is a real compliment when you know his ex-wife
